fact: May 6 is exactly 126 days into the year - that's like counting to 126 every single morning since January 1st!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for baby rabbits to pop out of their burrows for the very first time - they're about as big as tennis balls and twice as fluffy!
Cherry blossom petals are usually falling like pink snow in Japan around this time, creating carpets so thick you can't see the ground underneath.
The sun rises before 6 AM in most of Europe on this date - early enough that roosters are still rubbing their eyes!
May 6 falls in the middle of Taurus season, when the zodiac bull is supposedly making everyone extra stubborn about their favorite ice cream flavors.
Dandelions are often exploding into their fluffy seed heads right about now - one plant can shoot 2,000 tiny parachutes into the air!
In Australia, this date marks the heart of autumn when eucalyptus trees drop so many leaves that koalas have to wade through crunchy piles to find their favorites.
The daylight on May 6 lasts about 14 hours in northern cities like London - that's enough time to watch 7 movies back-to-back!
Tadpoles in ponds across North America are typically sprouting their back legs right around this time, looking like tiny aliens with wiggly tails.
May 6 sits exactly 46 days after the spring equinox - close enough that you can still feel winter's last sneaky cold snaps trying to crash the party.
Honeybees are usually working overtime collecting nectar from apple blossoms, and a single hive can bring home 9 pounds of the sweet stuff in just one day!
